questions.question: How does the Equality Act 2010 define sexual harassment?
options: Only physical contact that leaves a visible injury
Conduct that is unlawful only if the person objects to it at the time
Unwanted conduct of a sexual nature that violates a person's dignity, or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating or offensive environment
Any comment about work that a colleague happens to dislike

questions.question: In the definition of sexual harassment, what does the word 'unwanted' mean?
options: The conduct is not invited or welcomed by the person, and they need not say so out loud
The person disliked the tone of voice used but not the content
The conduct was against the organisation's dress code
The person must have complained about it in writing

questions.question: A worker excuses a sexual remark by saying it was 'only a joke' and no offence was meant. What does the course say about this?
options: Conduct can still be harassment because of its effect, so a lack of intent is not a defence
It means the conduct cannot be harassment because there was no intent
It only matters if the joke was repeated more than once
It is acceptable as long as most people found it funny

questions.question: When deciding whether conduct violates dignity or creates a hostile environment, what does the law take into account?
options: Only whether the person responsible admits they intended harm
Only the opinion of the manager on duty
Only whether other colleagues also found it offensive
The person's own perception, all the circumstances, and whether it is reasonable for the conduct to have that effect

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of verbal sexual harassment?
options: Offering to help a colleague carry equipment
Giving honest, respectful feedback about someone's work
Politely asking a colleague how their weekend was
Intrusive questions about a colleague's sex life and repeated unwanted requests to go on dates

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of non-verbal sexual harassment?
options: Explaining a care task to a new member of staff
Staring or leering, sexual gestures, or displaying sexual images
Waving hello to a colleague across the room
Asking a colleague to sign a handover sheet

questions.question: In a care setting, why is it especially important to be clear about the difference between necessary care and physical sexual harassment?
options: Because close personal contact is a normal part of the work, so the line between consented care and unwanted sexual conduct must be clear
Because physical harassment is not covered by the Equality Act
Because touching is never allowed in care at all
Because only registered nurses are permitted to give personal care

questions.question: Which of the following best describes online or 'digital' sexual harassment as covered in the course?
options: It only counts if it is sent from a work device
It cannot count as harassment because it happens on a screen
It is limited to comments made face to face
Sending sexual messages, images or emojis, or sharing explicit material in work group chats

questions.question: What is meant by 'third-party harassment'?
options: Harassment that only a third witness can confirm
Harassment reported to a third organisation such as a regulator
Harassment that is investigated by a third-party company
Harassment of care workers by the people they support, family members or other visitors, rather than colleagues

questions.question: A care worker is repeatedly subjected to unwanted sexual comments by a person's relative during home visits. Which statement is correct?
options: It only matters if the conduct comes from another member of staff
Nothing can be done unless the comments are made in writing
This can be third-party sexual harassment; the worker should not have to tolerate it and it should be reported to the employer
The worker must simply accept it because it comes from a family member, not a colleague

questions.question: When a person who uses services is subjected to unwanted sexual conduct, why may this also be a safeguarding matter?
options: Because the person may be an adult at risk, so safeguarding procedures must be followed as well
Because service users are never covered by the Equality Act
Because safeguarding only applies to staff, not service users
Because it removes the need to record anything

questions.question: What is the Equality Act 2010 described as in the course?
options: The main law protecting people from discrimination, harassment and victimisation in Great Britain
A policy written by each individual employer
A law that applies only to people at work, not to those using services
A voluntary code of practice for care homes only

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010?
options: Sexual orientation
Length of service
Choice of shift pattern
Job title

questions.question: For sexual harassment (unwanted conduct of a sexual nature) to apply, does the person affected have to share any particular protected characteristic?
options: Yes; the person must first prove their religion or belief
Yes; only people with a disability are protected
No; sexual harassment can happen to anyone, whoever they are
Yes; it applies only to harassment related to age

questions.question: Under the Equality Act 2010, what is victimisation?
options: Giving someone honest, fair feedback in an appraisal
Any disagreement between two colleagues about how to do their work
Asking a colleague to follow the organisation's policies
Treating someone badly because they have made, or supported, a complaint about harassment or discrimination

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a 'protected act' under the Equality Act 2010?
options: Choosing not to work overtime
Requesting a change to annual leave
Making a complaint of harassment, or giving evidence to support someone else's complaint
Refusing to attend a team meeting

questions.question: What duty did the Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023 introduce?
options: A preventative duty on employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of their workers
A duty on employees to sign a confidentiality agreement
A duty to remove all personal contact from care work
A duty on workers to investigate every complaint themselves

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a 'reasonable step' an employer might take to prevent sexual harassment?
options: Waiting for a tribunal claim before doing anything
Telling workers to keep concerns to themselves
Having a clear anti-harassment policy, providing training and safe reporting routes
Refusing to accept any reports of third-party harassment

questions.question: According to the course, what can happen at an employment tribunal where an employer has not taken reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment?
options: The employer is exempt from any further duty
The worker is required to pay the employer's costs
The claim is automatically dismissed
The tribunal can increase the compensation awarded in a sexual harassment claim

questions.question: Whose responsibility is it to help create a respectful culture where sexual harassment is not tolerated?
options: Only the person who has been harassed
Only the human resources department
Only external regulators
Everyone shares it; managers set the tone but every worker helps shape whether the workplace feels safe

questions.question: A colleague confides that they have been sexually harassed at work. What is the best way to respond?
options: Listen without judgement, believe them, reassure them, and be honest that you may need to pass the concern on
Promise to keep it completely secret and never tell anyone
Tell them to forget about it, as it was probably just a misunderstanding
Ask them why they did not do more to stop it at the time

questions.question: When someone tells you they have experienced sexual harassment, why should you avoid promising to keep it completely secret?
options: Because the person is unlikely to be telling the truth
Because secrets are always shared with the whole team
Because confidentiality is never important
Because you should not promise a secrecy you cannot keep, as the concern may need to be reported

questions.question: Who would normally be the right person to report a concern about sexual harassment to?
options: The person who carried out the harassment
A friend outside work who is not involved
Your manager, a designated person, or your safeguarding lead
No one, until you have gathered enough proof

questions.question: How should information about a sexual harassment concern be handled?
options: Kept entirely secret even where there is a safeguarding risk
Handled sensitively and shared only with those who need to know in order to act
Discussed openly with the whole team so people are warned
Posted in the staff group chat

questions.question: Which of these is named in the course as a source of external support relating to sexual harassment?
options: The person's next of kin
ACAS or the Equality and Human Rights Commission
A social media help page
The care worker's own family

questions.question: Conduct can amount to sexual harassment because of its effect on a person, even if the person responsible did not intend to cause any harm.
options: True
False

questions.question: Sexual harassment must be physical to count; verbal, non-verbal and online conduct are never included.
options: True
False

questions.question: A single serious incident can amount to sexual harassment; it does not have to be repeated.
options: True
False

questions.question: Because harassment by a client or a visitor is 'part of the job', a care worker simply has to tolerate it.
options: True
False

questions.question: The Worker Protection (Amendment of Equality Act 2010) Act 2023 places a duty on employers to take reasonable steps to prevent sexual harassment of their workers.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is a care worker's job to investigate a sexual harassment complaint and decide whether harassment has 'really' happened.
options: True
False

questions.question: Protection against victimisation is meant to help people speak up about harassment without fear of being punished for doing so.
options: True
False

questions.question: This awareness course replaces your organisation's policies, procedures and supervised training.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are forms sexual harassment can take, according to the course? (Select all that apply)
options: Giving fair, respectful feedback about someone's work
Verbal, such as sexual comments or jokes
Online, such as sexual messages or images
Non-verbal, such as staring, leering or gestures
Ordinary, respectful conversation between colleagues

questions.question: Which of the following could be examples of verbal or non-verbal sexual harassment? (Select all that apply)
options: Politely greeting a colleague at the start of a shift
Wolf-whistling or sexual gestures
Remarks about a person's body or appearance
Displaying or sharing sexual pictures

questions.question: When someone tells you they have experienced sexual harassment, which of these are supportive ways to respond? (Select all that apply)
options: Listen calmly and without judgement
Ask blaming questions about why they did not stop it
Tell them to forget about it as it was probably a misunderstanding
Reassure them that speaking up was the right thing to do
Believe them and take the concern seriously

questions.question: Which of the following are good practice when reporting a sexual harassment concern? (Select all that apply)
options: Share information only with those who need to know
Wait until you have gathered enough proof to be completely certain
Record the facts clearly using factual language
Report it promptly to your manager or safeguarding lead

questions.question: Which of the following are protected characteristics under the Equality Act 2010, as listed in the course? (Select all that apply)
options: Race
Disability
Number of years employed
Age
Job title

questions.question: Which of the following are reasonable steps an employer might take under the preventative duty? (Select all that apply)
options: Assessing and reducing the risks of harassment, including from third parties
Having a clear anti-harassment policy
Providing training
Discouraging workers from raising concerns

questions.question: Which of the following are named in the course as sources of support relating to sexual harassment? (Select all that apply)
options: Posting the details publicly on social media
ACAS or the Equality and Human Rights Commission
Your manager and your organisation's policies
The person who carried out the harassment
An employee assistance or occupational health service

questions.question: The Equality Act 2010 defines sexual harassment as unwanted conduct of a ___ nature.
Sexual harassment is judged by its ___, not by whether the person responsible intended to cause harm.
Harassment of care workers by clients, family members or visitors, rather than by colleagues, is often called ___-party harassment.
The Equality Act 2010 protects people from victimisation, which means being treated badly for doing a protected ___, such as making a complaint of harassment.
Where a person who uses services is subjected to unwanted sexual conduct and may be an adult at risk, this may also be a ___ matter, and you should follow those procedures.
